questions.q: Which axiom for $\mathcal{I} \subseteq 2^E$ distinguishes a matroid from an arbitrary simplicial complex (a downward-closed family containing $\emptyset$)?
choices: (I3) if $|I| < |J|$ for $I,J \in \mathcal{I}$ then some $e \in J\setminus I$ has $I\cup\{e\} \in \mathcal{I}$
questions.explain: (I1) and (I2) define a simplicial complex. (I3) — the exchange or augmentation axiom — is the matroid-specific content; it forces all maximal independent sets to have the same size.
questions.hint: A simplicial complex has a non-empty hereditary family by definition; what extra fact about sizes does a matroid demand?

questions.q: Take $E = \{a,b,c,d\}$ and let $\mathcal{I}$ contain $\emptyset$, every singleton, and $\{a,b\}$, $\{a,b,c\}$. Which axioms FAIL?
questions.explain: (I1) holds and (I2) holds (every subset of $\{a,b,c\}$ that's listed is closed under taking subsets among the listed sets). (I3) fails: take $I=\{c\}$ and $J=\{a,b\}$ — $|I|<|J|$ but neither $\{a,c\}$ nor $\{b,c\}$ is in $\mathcal{I}$, so no element of $J\setminus I$ extends $I$ to an independent set.
questions.hint: Look for two independent sets of different sizes whose smaller one cannot be augmented.

questions.q: Why does (I3) imply that all maximal independent sets in a matroid have the same cardinality?
choices: Because $\mathcal{I}$ is finite, so there's a unique largest size by definition
If $I, J$ are maximal with $|I| < |J|$, (I3) lets us extend $I$ — contradicting maximality
Because the rank function is constant on maximal independent sets by definition
questions.explain: Maximality means no element can be added without leaving $\mathcal{I}$. If $|I| < |J|$ then (I3) supplies an $e \in J \setminus I$ with $I \cup \{e\} \in \mathcal{I}$, contradicting $I$'s maximality. So all maxima have equal size — the rank.
questions.hint: Maximal means 'can't add anything'. (I3) says 'if you're smaller than someone, you can add something.'

questions.q: For the uniform matroid $U_{3,5}$ (ground set of size $5$, every $3$-subset is a basis), what is $r(\{1,2,4,5\})$?
questions.hint: The rank of a subset in $U_{r,n}$ caps at $r$.

questions.q: Which property of the rank function $r : 2^E \to \mathbb{Z}$ is the 'submodularity' inequality?
questions.explain: The first is the bounding axiom (R1), the second is monotonicity (R2), the third is submodularity (R3). The fourth is true (a definition) but not labelled 'submodularity'.
questions.hint: Submodularity always involves both $S \cup T$ and $S \cap T$ on one side.

questions.q: Two bases $B = \{a,b,c\}$ and $B' = \{a,d,e\}$ in some matroid. By basis exchange, swapping $b$ out of $B$, which element of $B' \setminus B$ is GUARANTEED to give a basis $(B \setminus \{b\}) \cup \{?\}$?
choices: Either $d$ or $e$ — at least one of them works (basis exchange)
None of $B' \setminus B$ is guaranteed; you need an explicit construction
questions.explain: Basis exchange: for any $x \in B \setminus B'$, there EXISTS $y \in B' \setminus B$ with $(B \setminus \{x\}) \cup \{y\}$ a basis. Existence — not 'all'. Here $B \setminus B' = \{b,c\}$ and $B' \setminus B = \{d,e\}$; some specific $y \in \{d,e\}$ works for the swap of $b$, but not both in general.
questions.hint: The axiom guarantees existence of a swap partner, not that every candidate works.

questions.q: How many bases (= spanning trees) does the graphic matroid $M(K_4)$ have? (Cayley's formula: $K_n$ has $n^{n-2}$ spanning trees.)
questions.explain: $K_4$ has $4^{4-2} = 4^2 = 16$ spanning trees, so $M(K_4)$ has 16 bases.
questions.hint: $K_n$ has $n^{n-2}$ spanning trees by Cayley's formula.

questions.q: Which is a vector matroid representation of $M(K_3)$ (the triangle, $3$ edges, rank $2$)?
choices: Columns $e_1, e_2, e_3$ — the standard basis of $\mathbb{R}^3$
questions.explain: The signed-incidence representation maps edge $\{i,j\}$ to $e_i - e_j$. The three vectors $e_1-e_2, e_1-e_3, e_2-e_3$ have rank $2$ (they sum to zero) and any two are linearly independent — exactly mirroring 'any two edges of $K_3$ form a forest, all three edges form a cycle.' The standard-basis option gives all-independent, which is $U_{3,3}$, not $M(K_3)$.
questions.hint: You want a configuration where any two columns are independent but all three columns are dependent — matching 'any two edges form a forest, all three form a triangle.'

questions.q: The Vámos matroid $V_8$ on $8$ elements is famous because:
choices: It has $V_8$ as a subgroup of automorphisms
It is not representable over any field
It is the smallest non-graphic matroid
It has the largest number of bases among rank-$4$ matroids on $8$ elements
questions.explain: $V_8$ is the canonical example of a matroid that fails to be a vector matroid over any field — even with characteristic $0$ allowed. In contrast, $U_{2,4}$ is non-representable over $\mathbb{F}_2$ but is fine over $\mathbb{R}$.
questions.hint: The point of $V_8$ is what cannot be done with it as a vector matroid.

questions.q: In the graphic matroid $M(G)$, what is $\operatorname{cl}(\{e\})$ for a single edge $e$ that is not a loop?
choices: All edges parallel to $e$ together with $e$ (i.e. all edges with the same endpoint pair)
All edges sharing a vertex with $e$
questions.explain: $\operatorname{cl}(\{e\})$ collects every $f$ with $r(\{e,f\}) = r(\{e\}) = 1$. That happens iff $\{e,f\}$ is dependent, iff $f$ has the same endpoints as $e$ — i.e. $f$ is parallel to $e$. So $\operatorname{cl}(\{e\}) = \{f : f$ parallel to $e\}$ (including $e$).
questions.hint: $f \in \operatorname{cl}(\{e\})$ means adding $f$ to $\{e\}$ doesn't increase the rank — i.e. $\{e,f\}$ is already dependent.

questions.q: Mac Lane–Steinitz exchange in closure form says: if $y \in \operatorname{cl}(S \cup \{x\}) \setminus \operatorname{cl}(S)$, then…
questions.explain: (C4): if adding $x$ to $S$ brings $y$ into the closure (and $y$ wasn't already there), then by symmetry adding $y$ to $S$ brings $x$ into the closure. This is the closure-side avatar of the exchange axiom — same content as (I3).
questions.hint: The axiom is a 'swap' between $x$ and $y$.

questions.q: Birkhoff's theorem (1935) identifies which two structures?
choices: Geometric lattices and simple matroids (i.e. matroids without loops or parallel elements)
questions.explain: Geometric lattices = graded, atomistic, semimodular lattices. Birkhoff showed these are exactly the lattices of flats of simple matroids — providing the third major cryptomorphism (alongside bases ↔ rank ↔ closure).
questions.hint: The result is matroid-specific; one option is about general posets, the rest are not.

questions.q: The dual of the uniform matroid $U_{3,7}$ is $U_{?,7}$. What is the missing first index?
questions.explain: Bases of $U_{r,n}$ are the $r$-subsets; complementing each gives the $(n-r)$-subsets, which are the bases of $U_{n-r,n}$. So $(U_{3,7})^* = U_{4,7}$.
questions.hint: Replace each basis by its complement and read off the new basis size.

questions.q: If $M$ is a rank-$3$ matroid on a $7$-element ground set, what is $r(M^*)$?
questions.hint: There is a clean rank identity linking $r(M)$, $r(M^*)$, and $|E|$.

questions.q: Whitney's planarity theorem says: a graph $G$ is planar if and only if…
choices: $M(G)^*$ is graphic — i.e. equals $M(G^*)$ for some graph $G^*$
$M(G)$ has the largest possible number of bases among graphs on $|V(G)|$ vertices
questions.explain: Planarity is a matroid-theoretic statement about $M(G)$'s dual: $M(G)^*$ is graphic iff $G$ is planar, with the planar dual $G^*$ providing the witness graph. This subsumes Kuratowski's $K_5$/$K_{3,3}$ criterion.
questions.hint: The criterion is about whether the dual matroid is itself a graphic matroid.

questions.q: The Rado–Edmonds theorem says that for a non-empty hereditary family $\mathcal{I}$ on a finite set, the greedy algorithm finds a maximum-weight member of $\mathcal{I}$ for every non-negative weight function if and only if…
choices: $\mathcal{I}$ satisfies the exchange axiom (I3) — i.e. is a matroid
All members of $\mathcal{I}$ have the same cardinality
questions.explain: Rado–Edmonds: the optimality of greedy for ALL weights is exactly equivalent to $(E, \mathcal{I})$ being a matroid. So matroids are characterized by the success of the greedy algorithm.
questions.hint: The theorem gives an iff between greedy-correctness and one of the matroid axioms.

questions.q: Kruskal's algorithm computes a minimum-spanning-tree. Which matroid is it the greedy algorithm for?
choices: The graphic matroid $M(G)$ with edge weights
The transversal matroid of the bipartite graph $V \times E$
questions.explain: Kruskal sorts edges by weight (ascending — equivalent to greedy on $-w$) and adds each if it doesn't create a cycle, i.e. if independence in $M(G)$ is preserved. Output: a minimum spanning tree = a min-weight basis of $M(G)$.
questions.hint: Kruskal's 'add an edge if it doesn't form a cycle' is exactly an independence test in which matroid?

questions.q: Edmonds' matroid intersection theorem (1970) shows polynomial-time algorithms for the maximum common independent set in TWO matroids on a shared ground set. What is the analogous problem for THREE matroids?
choices: Equivalent to matroid duality, hence polynomial
questions.explain: Two-matroid intersection captures bipartite matching, arborescences, and more in polynomial time. Three-matroid intersection encodes 3-dimensional matching, an NP-complete problem. Two is the boundary of tractability.
questions.hint: Three-dimensional matching is NP-complete — and three-matroid intersection encodes it.

questions.q: What does $T_M(1,1)$ equal?
choices: The number of independent sets of $M$
The chromatic polynomial of $G$ at $1$, when $M = M(G)$
questions.explain: Substituting $x=y=1$ into $\sum_S (x-1)^{r(E)-r(S)}(y-1)^{|S|-r(S)}$ kills every term except those with both exponents zero: $r(E)=r(S)$ and $|S|=r(S)$. The first means $S$ spans, the second means $S$ is independent — together, $S$ is a basis. So $T_M(1,1) = $ number of bases.
questions.hint: At $(1,1)$ both shift factors vanish unless their exponents are both $0$ — and that pins down which subsets contribute.

questions.q: The deletion–contraction recursion for $T_M$ at a non-loop, non-coloop element $e$ is:
questions.explain: When $e$ is neither a loop nor a coloop, $T_M = T_{M \setminus e} + T_{M/e}$ — additive. Loops give $T_M = y \cdot T_{M \setminus e}$ and coloops give $T_M = x \cdot T_{M/e}$. The polynomial form is independent of the order in which elements are deleted/contracted.
questions.hint: It's an additive split — and the same template as the deletion–contraction for the chromatic polynomial.

questions.q: How many proper $3$-colorings does $K_3$ have? (Equivalently, evaluate the chromatic polynomial $P_{K_3}(k) = k(k-1)(k-2)$ — the Tutte specialization $k^{c(G)} (-1)^{r(M)} T_{M(G)}(1-k, 0)$ — at $k=3$.)
questions.explain: The chromatic polynomial of $K_3$ is $P_{K_3}(k) = k(k-1)(k-2)$: $k$ choices for vertex 1, $k-1$ for vertex 2, $k-2$ for vertex 3. At $k=3$ this gives $3 \cdot 2 \cdot 1 = 6$. As a Tutte specialization with $T_{M(K_3)}(x,y) = x^2 + x + y$, $r=2$, $c=1$: $P_{K_3}(k) = k(-1)^2 T(1-k, 0) = k[(1-k)^2 + (1-k)] = k(1-k)(2-k)$, and $P(3) = 3 \cdot (-2) \cdot (-1) = 6$. ✓
questions.hint: Number the vertices and pick colors in order: $k \cdot (k-1) \cdot (k-2)$.
